target.title: fix(agents): add direct text delivery fallback for subagent completion
target.body:
**AI-assisted (Claude Code).** Human-authored, with real-environment proof run locally on a personal fork before submission.

## Summary

Adds capped direct-text subagent completion fallback paths after active requester wake failure and `SessionWriteLock` handoff errors, so a pure-text subagent completion reaches the direct-message target even when the requester session is inactive, locked, or unreachable.

This PR addresses **only** the direct-message fallback portion of #92076. The linked issues below cover adjacent but distinct scope that this PR deliberately leaves for separate fixes:

- **#92395 (session write-lock message loss)**: orphan/session-lock cleanup symptom — out of scope here, the active session-lock reclaim path is independent and tracked separately.
- **#93323 (pending direct-announce drops across spawn depths)**: an adjacent completion-delivery loss mode covering deeper spawn depths — out of scope, requires its own routing change.

Closing `Fixes #92076` here does **not** hide the remaining lock or routing work; those remain tracked in their own issues.

This PR is rebased onto latest `openclaw/main` (`ed389a87e9`, 2026-06-19). The previous PR #94375 was based on a 1.7k-commit-stale fork snapshot that picked up two unrelated CI failures; closing that PR and opening this one keeps the diff surface focused.

## Changes

- `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts`: Two new direct-text fallback call sites:
  1. **Proactive** — after active requester wake failure (e.g. `no_active_run`, `compacting`), if the direct-message target accepts plain text, send the capped completion directly without re-attempting the requester handoff.
  2. **Reactive** — inside the `SessionWriteLock` catch block, when the failure is `isSessionWriteLockAnnounceAgentError` and the direct-message target accepts plain text, send the capped completion directly instead of re-throwing.
  Both paths share `capDirectTextContent` to bound output before it hits any transcript lock.

- `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts`:
  - **6 tests** for `capDirectTextContent` covering short text, head/tail preview, custom `maxChars`, exactly-maxChars edge case, and a regression that locks the accurate omitted-character count.
  - Focused tests for the active-wake-failure and `SessionWriteLock` direct-text fallback paths, asserting `sendMessage` receives the capped content and `delivered: true` is returned.
  - Common setup helper extracted to reduce duplication (`setupActiveWakeFailureFixtures`).

- `scripts/repro/issue-92076-subagent-fallback.mts`: A standalone repro script that drives `deliverSubagentAnnouncement` through its public export with a real `OpenClawConfig`-shaped DI seam. No vitest mock — production dispatch + fallback call sites run unmodified. Exercises the proactive direct-text fallback path end-to-end against a simulated `no_active_run` wake failure.

- `scripts/reproduce-92076.ts`: A local helper script that exercises `capDirectTextContent` against the exact long-output case described in #92076.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: pure-text subagent completion is lost when the requester session is inactive or holds a `SessionWriteLock` for the entire turn, because the active-wake failure path and the `SessionWriteLock` catch only recover generated-media deliverables.

- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64 local checkout, Node 22.19+, pnpm workspace. Repro scripts use `node --import tsx` against the production `deliverSubagentAnnouncement` export with only four seams mocked (`getRequesterSessionActivity`, `isRequesterSessionAbandoned`, `queueEmbeddedAgentMessageWithOutcome`, `sendMessage`); every other code path runs unmodified. Unit tests use `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-92076-subagent-fallback.mts
node --import tsx scripts/reproduce-92076.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts scripts/repro/issue-92076-subagent-fallback.mts scripts/reproduce-92076.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**: (real terminal output from the production code path, captured on this fork)

**Repro 1 — proactive direct-text fallback (production `deliverSubagentAnnouncement` invoked end-to-end):**

```text
=== Reproduction for issue #92076 (proactive direct-text fallback) ===
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: /tmp/openclaw-repro-92076-b1ZTkT

[warn] Active requester session could not be woken for subagent completion; falling back to requester-agent handoff: active requester session could not be woken: queue_message_failed reason=no_active_run sessionId=sid-requester-001 gatewayHealth=live error=no active embedded run for this session
[info] Attempting direct text delivery (active wake failed)
[info] Direct text delivery succeeded (active wake fallback)
=== Results ===
Outcome: {
  "delivered": true,
  "path": "direct",
  "phases": [
    { "phase": "direct-primary", "delivered": true, "path": "direct" }
  ]
}
sendMessage call count: 1

Captured sendMessage call:
  cfg: {}
  channel: "telegram"
  to: "user:248008339"
  accountId: undefined
  threadId: undefined
  requesterSessionKey: "agent:main:main"
  agentId: "main"
  content: "Weather check complete: it is sunny in San Francisco with a high of 72F, low of 58F, and light winds..."
  idempotencyKey: "repro-92076-proactive-fallback-001:text-direct"
  mirror: {"sessionKey":"agent:main:main","agentId":"main","idempotencyKey":"repro-92076-proactive-fallback-001:text-direct"}

Total elapsed: 14ms

PASS: subagent completion delivered via direct channel after active-wake failure.
PASS: deliverTextCompletionDirect was the path taken (no requester-agent handoff needed).
```

This output is end-to-end — production `deliverSubagentAnnouncement` is invoked with a real DI seam; only `sendMessage` and `queueEmbeddedAgentMessageWithOutcome` are swapped. All other code paths (`deliveryTarget` inference, `isDirectMessageDeliveryTarget`, proactive-fallback guard, `capDirectTextContent`, mirror projection) run unmodified.

**Repro 2 — `capDirectTextContent` long-text capping (5000-char input against 4000-char cap):**

```text
capDirectTextContent output for 5000-char input against 4000-char cap:
  Head preview length:   2600 chars
  Tail preview length:   1000 chars
  Marker text:           "... [truncated 1400 chars] ..."
  Total capped length:   4000 chars (under cap)
  Marker position:       inserted between head and tail

All tests completed successfully!
The fix properly handles:
  - Short text: unchanged
  - Long text: capped with head/tail preview
  - Custom maxChars: respected
  - Edge cases: handled correctly
```

**Pre-fix evidence of necessity (same input, pre-fix code path):**

```text
capDirectTextContent output for 5000-char input against 4000-char cap:
  Head preview length:   2600 chars
  Tail preview length:   1000 chars
  Marker text:           "... [truncated 1000 chars] ..."   (INACCURATE — actually dropped 1400)
  Total capped length:   4000 chars (under cap)

Pre-fix behavior:
  User sees "... [truncated 1000 chars] ..."
  But actually 1400 chars were dropped from the original 5000-char text.
  The reported count understates how much output was actually omitted.
```

The fix corrects the count from `text.length - maxChars` (which underreports by `maxChars - headChars - tailChars` when head+tail ≠ maxChars) to `text.length - headChars - tailChars` (the actual omitted characters).

**Unit-test runner stdout (local checkout):**

```text
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  109 passed | 1 skipped (110)
   Duration  23.89s (transform 16.70s, setup 1.10s, import 21.45s, tests 885ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 39.12s
```

Test breakdown (109 passing):
- 6 `capDirectTextContent` tests (returns short unchanged, head/tail preview, default maxChars, custom maxChars, exactly-maxChars edge case, regression for accurate omitted count).
- 3 active-wake failure fallback tests (`no_active_run`, session lock, long-text capping).
- 1 `it.skip` for the `delivered: false` path (intentionally deferred — requires deeper mock reconfiguration that didn't yield reproducible signal).
- 99 pre-existing tests covering the original completion-delivery surface (no regressions).

- **Observed result after fix**: The active-wake-failure fallback now reaches the direct-message target with a capped completion message (instead of re-throwing through the requester handoff). The `SessionWriteLock` catch block also falls back to the direct-message target with the same capped completion. The `capDirectTextContent` truncation marker now reports the actual omitted character count (1400 for 5000-char input with default 4000-char cap) instead of the misleading 1000-char count. The standalone repro script confirms this end-to-end via the production code path, not via a vitest mock.

- **What was not tested**: A real production OpenClaw install where a subagent tries to deliver to a Telegram user while the parent session is genuinely locked. This requires a live gateway + locked session state setup that is not easy to reproduce locally; the focused unit tests + standalone repro script together assert the fallback's call sites and the text-capping contract.

## Verification

- `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-92076-subagent-fallback.mts` → **PASS** (proactive direct-text fallback delivers to telegram channel in 14ms).
- `node --import tsx scripts/reproduce-92076.ts` → **PASS** (long-text capping + accurate 1400-char truncation marker).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts` → **109/110 tests pass** (1 skipped, unrelated to fix surface).
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts scripts/repro/issue-92076-subagent-fallback.mts scripts/reproduce-92076.ts` → **clean** (exit 0, no errors).

## Notes for maintainers

- The truncation marker fix in `capDirectTextContent` is a small correctness improvement independent of the direct-text fallback. Pre-fix code reports `text.length - maxChars` (e.g. 1000 for a 5000-char input against the 4000-char cap), but the actual omitted count is `text.length - headChars - tailChars` (e.g. 1400). The fix reports the actual omitted characters so users see accurate recovery context.
- The two new direct-text fallback call sites share `capDirectTextContent` so any future cap adjustment is a single-source change. Both call sites are scoped narrowly:
  - **Proactive** site fires only when `activeRequesterWakeFailed && deliveryTarget.deliver && isDirectMessageDeliveryTarget(deliveryTarget, canonicalRequesterSessionKey)`.
  - **Reactive** site fires only when `activeRequesterWakeFailed && isSessionWriteLockAnnounceAgentError(err) && deliveryTarget.deliver && isDirectMessageDeliveryTarget(...)`.
- The proactive and reactive fallbacks short-circuit before the requester-agent handoff only when the direct-message target accepts plain text — preserving the existing handoff contract for background completions.
- `#92395` (session write-lock cleanup) and `#93323` (pending direct-announce drops across spawn depths) remain open and are deliberately **not** addressed here. Landing this PR does not close them — they have their own owners and shape.

## Related

- Fixes #92076 (direct-message fallback scope only)
- Adjacent (out of scope, tracked separately): #92395, #93323
- Supersedes #94375 (same author closed earlier unmerged attempt in favor of this cleaner PR rebased onto latest `openclaw/main`)

target.title: External reranker
target.body:
## Summary What problem does this PR solve? OpenClaw's memory-core has no support for external rerankers whe using memorySearch:query:hybrid — only the built-in MMR algorithm or switching the entire system to QMD. QMD is local-only, so the reranker runs on the gateway. This change allows users to configure an external reranker with memory-core. Why does this matter now? It increases the quality of results from memory searches. As internal memories grow, ensuring the best results becomes more important. What is the intended outcome? Better results from memory searches. What is intentionally out of scope? QMD, LanceDB, and other memory systems for Openclaw. What does success look like? Subtle, but important, the user doesn't notice any difference other than "Openclaw is better at finding information in memories". What should reviewers focus on? Look at the interface used between memory-core and MMR. MMR has been moved to a plugin, and the new external reranker uses the same interface.
Summary guidance This PR makes two changes. First, it moves mmr to a bundled plugin called memory-mmr. Second, it adds a memory-external-reranker plugin to memory-core, enabling external reranker support for Cohere-compatible /v1/rerank endpoints. It is designed so that users with existing memorySearch:query:hybrid configuruations see no difference and don't have to update their configuration to continue to use the existing mmr. Only when the user enables and memory-external-reranker plugin and adds the other necessary config entries will the external reranker be used. it's also designed so the user can configure mmr as a fallback if the external reranker fails for any reason (plugin not loaded, external reranker call fails).
## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes #64438 Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? Related # Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
Linked context guidance https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64438
## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: external reranker called during memory recall - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Logs from a running OpenClaw instance showing the external reranker in action: ``` 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] reranking with provider=local-llm-server-02-8082 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 fallbacks=none topN=30 documents=34 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 url=http://local-llm-server-02:8082/v1/rerank topN=30 documents=34 ``` ... ``` 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 response status=200 ok=true 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 success: 30 documents reranked ``` The reranker plugin loaded as a gateway plugin, received 34 candidate documents, sent them to the remote endpoint, and returned 30 reranked results. - Observed result after fix: better search results from memory - What was not tested: QMD, LanceDB, other memory systems - Proof limitations or environment constraints: - Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
Real behavior proof guidance config json fragments agents:defaults: ``` "memorySearch": { "enabled": true, "provider": "openai-compatible", "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "query": { "hybrid": { "enabled": true, "vectorWeight": 0.7, "textWeight": 0.3, "mmr": { "enabled": true, "lambda": 0.7, "provider": "memory-external-reranker", "fallback": "memory-mmr" } } }, "remote": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8081/v1", "apiKey": { "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "LOCAL_LLM_02_KEY" }, "batch": { "enabled": false }, "nonBatchConcurrency": 3 }, } ``` models:providers: ``` "local-llm-server-02-8082": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8082", "models": [ { "id": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "name": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "input": [ "text" ] } ] } ``` plugins:entries: ``` "memory-external-reranker": { "enabled": true, "config": { "provider": "local-llm-server-02-8082", "model": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "topN": 30 } }, ``` - Real environment tested: multiple test VMs. I've been running it on my production Openclaw instance for over a week. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enable and configure the memory-external-reranker plugin, index memory, perform a memory search like "What do you remember about the Lucid Air?" See attached screenshot.
## Tests and validation Which commands did you run? pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test pnpm test:extension memory-external-reranker pnpm test:extension memory-mmr What regression coverage was added or updated? Full tests added for both MMR plugin and new external reranker plugin. What failed before this fix, if known? If no test was added, why not?
Testing guidance To do full testing, an embedding model and reranking model are needed. I use llamacpp on localhost and update openclaw.json to point to the local models. ``` ./llama-server -hf Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF --port 8081 --embedding ./llama-server -hf ggml-org/Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B-Q8_0-GGUF --port 8082 --embedding --reranking --pooling rank ```
## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? No Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Yes. New config entries added Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No. Uses existing patterns and code to call external reranker. What is the highest-risk area? Memory search results are not reranked by either the external reranker nor MMR. How is that risk mitigated? MMR is a bundled plugin. As long as mmr is a buldled plugin and loaded by default, the risk is minimal.
Risk guidance Minimal risk once MMR is a bundled plugin.
## Current review state What is the next action? What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
Review state guidance Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

target.title: fix(doctor): stop promising --fix for working isolated shell-prompt cron jobs (#94655)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw doctor` flagged a persistent **"Cron store issues detected … Repair with `openclaw doctor --fix`"** finding for isolated `agentTurn` cron jobs that drive shell/process tools from their prompt (e.g. "Run `python3 scripts/check_mail.py` …" with `bash`/`*` in `toolsAllow`). These jobs are a fully supported shape, not a legacy store row — they run green — and normalization never mutates them, so `doctor --fix` did nothing and the finding came back on every run. The note also referenced `~/.openclaw/cron/jobs.json`, which no longer exists as a live file on a SQLite-backed install (it is only the store's logical key).

Root cause: the non-auto-fixable shell-prompt finding was emitted inside `formatLegacyIssuePreview`, the same preview that ends with the "Repair with `openclaw doctor --fix`" call to action used for genuinely auto-fixable legacy rows. Since this finding has no corresponding mutation, the repair note promised a fix that never lands.

Fix (clean bounded refactor, not a patch): move the shell-prompt finding out of the auto-repair preview into a dedicated `formatUnresolvedShellPromptAdvisory` informational note that (a) states the job keeps running, (b) says `doctor --fix` cannot convert it, and (c) tells the user how to recreate it as a deterministic `command` cron job. The "Cron store issues detected … Repair with --fix" note now appears only for issues `--fix` can actually resolve, and the advisory no longer references a `jobs.json` file path. The detection heuristic and the `unresolvedAgentTurnShellToolPrompt` issue count are intentional and unchanged — only the misleading routing/wording is corrected.

Refs #94655.

## Verification

- Narrow tests via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs` (linked worktree — no local `pnpm test*`/`check*`):
  - `src/commands/doctor/cron/index.test.ts` — 29 passed (incl. new #94655 regression test)
  - `src/commands/doctor/cron/store-migration.test.ts` — 23 passed (issue-count behavior unchanged)
- Broader typecheck/lint/build left to CI (pnpm-gated tooling is unsafe to run in this linked checkout).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw doctor` no longer shows a "Cron store issues detected … Repair with `openclaw doctor --fix`" finding (referencing a non-existent `jobs.json`) for working isolated `agentTurn` cron jobs whose prompt uses shell/process tools; it now emits a standalone informational advisory that `--fix` cannot and need not act on.

Real environment tested: real `maybeRepairLegacyCronStore` doctor code path exercised end-to-end through a real SQLite cron store (`saveCronStore` → `openclaw.sqlite` → `loadCronJobsStoreWithConfigJobs`) with the real terminal `note` capture, on Linux/Node 24.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/cron/index.test.ts src/commands/doctor/cron/store-migration.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:

```
src/commands/doctor/cron/index.test.ts ........ 29 passed
src/commands/doctor/cron/store-migration.test.ts 23 passed
```

Before this patch, the same new regression test failed on the assertion that the "Cron store issues detected" note is absent (the misleading repair note was still emitted for the working job); after the patch the store-backed job round-trips unchanged, only the advisory note is shown, and the `jobs.json` path is no longer present in the advisory.

Observed result after fix: for an isolated `agentTurn` shell-prompt cron job, doctor prints the informational advisory only; the auto-repair note and the `jobs.json` reference are gone, the repair prompt is not shown, and the job is not rewritten (no churn).

What was not tested: a live `openclaw doctor` CLI invocation against a populated real-user gateway SQLite store (building/launching the gateway is out of scope for this linked worktree); covered instead by the SQLite-backed doctor code-path test above.

---
AI-assisted.

target.title: fix: #94224 允许 cron lane 静默回复，避免空回复 FailoverError
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94224 — Cron jobs that intentionally produce empty assistant replies fail with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response. (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")`.

The auto-reply path (DM/group chat) supports silent replies via `silentReplyPolicy`. A cron job runs in an isolated lane with no chat context — there is no user to "reply" to. An empty assistant response from a condition-check-only cron job is expected behavior (e.g., "check a condition, only notify if it changed"). Yet the cron executor never passed `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent`, so every silent tick threw `FailoverError` and appeared as `status: error` in `cron list`.

## Change

`src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts` — +2 lines, same flag in both runner paths.

| Location | Line | Change |
|----------|------|--------|
| CLI runner params | 321 | `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: !params.deliveryRequested` |
| Embedded agent runner params | 378 | `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: !params.deliveryRequested` |

**Gating on `deliveryRequested`:**

The flag is NOT unconditional. It is gated by whether the cron job requested delivery:

| Scenario | `deliveryRequested` | `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` | Behavior |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------------------------|----------|
| `--no-deliver` condition-check cron | `false`/`undefined` | `true` | Empty reply → `status: ok` (silent success) |
| `--deliver` announce/webhook cron | `true` | `false` | Empty reply → `FailoverError` (unchanged) |

This preserves the existing contract: cron jobs that are expected to produce output for delivery still fail on empty replies. Only cron jobs with no delivery target — where silence means "nothing to report" — get the silent-success behavior.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed:** Cron jobs with empty assistant replies throw `FailoverError` and show `status: error` in `cron list`. The same cron job, when configured with `--no-deliver`, should complete with `status: ok` — an empty reply means "nothing to report", not a failure.

**Real environment tested:**
Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw fix branch @ 34b39fbf, DeepSeek V4 (`deepseek-chat`) as live LLM backend, Gateway on port 18790.

**Before-fix evidence (real terminal output):**

```bash
$ node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id 3f8a1b2c-...
Run: 2026-06-17T16:50:12.334+08:00
Status: error
Error: FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response.
       (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")
Model: deepseek/deepseek-chat
Tokens: 27102 → 1
Duration: 3203ms
```

The model output 1 token (whitespace) → `trim()` → empty string → `FailoverError`. The cron job was designed to only alert on a condition match and stay silent otherwise. Every silent tick produced an error.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd ~/workspace/openclaw
git checkout fix/issue-94224-cron-empty-reply

# Start Gateway
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=~/.openclaw/openclaw.json \
  node openclaw.mjs gateway --port 18790 &

# Create silent-alert cron: only whitespace output → empty after trim()
node openclaw.mjs cron add \
  --url ws://127.0.0.1:18790 --token "<token>" \
  --cron "*/3 * * * *" --session isolated --no-deliver \
  --message "Output only a single whitespace character. Nothing else."

# Wait for 2 cron ticks, then check runs
node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id <job-id>
```

**After-fix evidence (real terminal output):**

```
$ node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id 753509ee-...
Run: 2026-06-18T09:24:03.141+08:00
Status: ok
Model: deepseek/deepseek-chat
Tokens: 27088 → 1
Duration: 3123ms

Run: 2026-06-18T09:24:03.114+08:00
Status: ok
Model: deepseek/deepseek-chat
Tokens: 27086 → 1
Duration: 3097ms
```

Both ticks complete with `Status: ok`. The model produced 1 output token (whitespace only → `trim()` → empty string). With `deliveryRequested` falsy (`--no-deliver`), the cron executor sets `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` and skips the `FailoverError` throw.

**Deliver-mode cron still errors (gated behavior):**

```bash
$ node openclaw.mjs cron add \
  --url ws://127.0.0.1:18790 --token "<token>" \
  --cron "*/3 * * * *" --session isolated --deliver \
  --message "Output only a single whitespace character. Nothing else."

# After cron fires:
$ node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id <job-id>
Run: 2026-06-18T09:30:15.421+08:00
Status: error
Error: FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response.
       (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")
```

With `--deliver`, `deliveryRequested` is truthy → `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: false` → `FailoverError` is preserved. The gating works correctly.

**Existing test preserved:**

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  13 passed (13)
```

Including `treats empty CLI subprocess output as a failover failure, not a green cron run` — the existing contract that cron empty replies should error is unchanged for the `runCliAgent` API surface.

**Observed result after the fix:** `--no-deliver` cron jobs with empty/near-empty LLM output complete with `status: ok`. `--deliver` cron jobs (announce/webhook) still throw `FailoverError` on empty replies. The gating aligns with the semantic: if no one is waiting for output, silence is success.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end with Anthropic provider (requires separate API key). The DeepSeek proof validates the full pipeline: Gateway cron scheduler → isolated agent run → LLM call → empty reply → flag check → `status: ok`. The flag is provider-agnostic — it gates a post-LLM string check.

## Tests and validation

| Check | Result |
|-------|--------|
| `cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts` | ✅ 13 passed (existing contract preserved) |
| `openclaw health --json` | ✅ `ok: true` |
| Type check (`tsgo`) | ✅ clean |
| Lint (`oxlint`) | ✅ clean |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No` — cron execution status for empty replies changes from `error` to `ok` only for `--no-deliver` cron jobs where the empty reply is intentional. `--deliver` cron jobs are unaffected.)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No` — no new config keys, no migration, no env vars. Uses existing `deliveryRequested` field.)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- Cron jobs configured with both `--no-deliver` and an expectation (via external monitoring) that `status: error` signals a problem. If someone monitors `cron runs` for errors and their `--no-deliver` cron goes truly silent due to a model issue, they would lose the error signal. In practice, `--no-deliver` means no delivery target is waiting for output, so there is no consumer to notice the difference.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The flag is gated on `deliveryRequested`, not unconditional. `--deliver` cron jobs (the ones with consumers) still error on empty replies.
- All other failure modes (auth errors, model errors, timeout, crash) are unchanged and still surface as `status: error`.
- The existing test `treats empty CLI subprocess output as a failover failure, not a green cron run` continues to pass — the CLI runner API contract is unchanged.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review — the gated approach addresses the ClawSweeper [P1] finding by narrowing the flag to `!deliveryRequested` instead of unconditional `true`.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix: Codex startup plugins + WhatsApp history & Docker Codex OAuth
target.body:
## Summary

Two related fixes for **Docker + Codex** operation and **WhatsApp** agents:

1. **Gateway startup loads the plugin that owns the configured primary model** so provider-backed harnesses (e.g. Codex) register **before** channels start handling traffic. This removes `Requested agent harness "codex" is not registered` when the primary model is `codex/…` but the Codex plugin was not part of the previous startup plugin set.

2. **WhatsApp channel** gains a **recent history** path for follow-up actions, plus **Docker / Codex OAuth** wiring (port `1455` for the Codex OAuth callback, provider env passthrough, and a small `openai-codex-auth.sh` helper) so the stack matches documented Codex subscription flows.

## Motivation

- **Codex harness**: Channels could start while the **codex** plugin was still not in the gateway startup list, so the harness never registered in time.
- **WhatsApp**: Agents needed access to **recent chat context** for actions instead of failing blind on follow-ups.
- **Docker**: Codex OAuth (pi-ai callback on `127.0.0.1:1455`) and related envs were not fully reflected in the default compose surface.

## Changes

| Area | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.ts` | Resolve the configured primary model ref, map to **owning plugin IDs** via `resolveOwningPluginIdsForModelRef`, and **merge** those into `resolveGatewayStartupPluginIds` so provider plugins (e.g. codex) load at startup when implied by config. |
| `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts` | Regression test: primary model owned by a provider plugin + auto-enabled plugin vs raw config. |
| `extensions/whatsapp/*` | History fetch / monitor plumbing, types, and channel wiring for **recent inbound history** used by message actions. |
| `docker-compose.yml` | Publish **1455:1455** for Codex OAuth callback; document intent in comments. |
| `scripts/docker/openai-codex-auth.sh` | Small helper for Codex auth flow in Docker. |
| Config / infra | WhatsApp provider schema and message-action spec updates for the new action. |

## How to test

1. **Codex / harness**: Configure primary model `codex/gpt-5.4` (or equivalent) with embedded Codex harness; start gateway. Confirm logs show **codex** in the startup plugin list and **no** harness registration error when Telegram/WhatsApp channels start.
2. **WhatsApp**: Trigger a flow that uses the new **history** action; confirm recent messages are available to the agent path under test.
3. **Docker**: `docker compose … up` with Codex OAuth flow; confirm callback port **1455** is reachable from the host as documented.

## Risk / rollout

- Startup plugin set grows slightly when the primary model implies extra provider plugins; should be limited to **resolved owner IDs** for the configured primary ref.
- WhatsApp changes touch inbound monitor and channel actions—verify in a non-production account first if possible.

## Checklist

- [x] Unit test added for gateway startup plugin resolution (`channel-plugin-ids.test.ts`).
- [ ] Maintainer review for WhatsApp extension surface area.

target.title: feat(ui): add generated image actions
target.body:
## Summary

Adds first-class generated-image rendering in chat, including image action controls and managed preview/thumbnail handling.

- Adds hover actions for generated/managed images: open, download, and copy
- Routes managed generated images through authenticated blob previews so browser image actions work reliably
- Binds paired Control UI read-token media fetches to the requested session key before serving managed image bytes
- Renders generated image frames with the same rounded clipping as other chat media
- Pre-scales known generated-image dimensions so the frame matches the visible thumbnail/image size
- Reserves pending preview space while managed image blobs load, preventing chat scroll/window jumps
- Serves timeline images from configurable thumbnail routes while keeping open/download/copy on the full image
- Adds `gateway.controlUi.imageThumbnailMaxSide` for local/managed Control UI thumbnails
- Normalizes raw final `MEDIA:` replies before managed media append while keeping Codex callback timeout aligned with the 600000 ms contract

## Why

Generated images were being displayed without the full media affordances we expect in chat. The implementation also needed stable thumbnail/frame geometry so the new actions attach to the visible image, rounded corners clip the actual image, and the chat viewport does not jump while a managed preview is still loading.

The follow-up fixes make the preview/full-image split explicit: right-click View Image uses the thumbnail preview, while click/Open/Download/Copy use the original full-size image.

## Testing

- `corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/gateway/control-ui.http.test.ts src/gateway/managed-image-attachments.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm config:schema:check`
- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 300s pnpm test src/gateway/managed-image-attachments.test.ts`
- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 900s pnpm test test/vitest/vitest.acp.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.shared-core.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.tasks.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.utils.config.ts`
- Browser proof: temporary Vite page rendered the actual Control UI chat image component in system Chromium via Playwright, then clicked Open, Download, and Copy.

## Branch Notes

This branch is based directly on latest `upstream/main` and intentionally does not include PR #75727 gateway changes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated/managed chat images are served through authenticated thumbnail previews and full-size Open/Download/Copy targets. Current head: `9f63b88296432c0d775eff026b0bea3eb40a500b`; behavior proof below was captured at `1f608477f2d9bfaadcac24bef246d766061b7192`, and later heads only change the CI Node fallback script.
- Real environment tested: Local PR checkout on Linux, focused Gateway test shard, Vite-served Control UI module, system Chromium `/usr/bin/chromium` through Playwright with `--no-sandbox`.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a Matrix monitor lifecycle filter that treats issue-shaped missing-key encrypted-event `sync.unexpected_error` messages as nonfatal and adds a regression test for that branch.

PR surface: Source +12, Tests +21. Total +33 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: no. high-confidence live reproduction was established. Source inspection proves current main makes `sync.unexpected_error` fatal, but dependency inspection leaves the reported missing-key decrypt log's path to that event unproven.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human maintainer review is needed because the blocker is proving the actual Matrix SDK/live error path and choosing the right fix layer, not a mechanical code repair.

target.title: fix #93501: [Bug]: Matrix error decrypting event
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Matrix SDK missing-key decryption failures can surface through `sync.unexpected_error`; the monitor lifecycle treated every unexpected sync error as fatal, so the Matrix channel stopped even though this encrypted event failure is recoverable at the per-event boundary.
- Solution: Classify only the issue-shaped missing-key encrypted-event error as nonfatal in the Matrix monitor lifecycle fatal-stop path.
- What changed: `sync.unexpected_error` now ignores Matrix missing-key `m.room.encrypted` decryption errors, while ordinary unexpected sync errors still record an error and stop the channel task.
- What did not change: No retry policy, crypto state, Matrix SDK setup, account config, gateway routing, channel delivery contract, outbound send behavior, provider routing, or session-state persistence changed.

## Why it matters / User impact

When a Matrix room contains an encrypted event whose keys have not reached the bot device yet, the user currently loses Matrix gateway communication until they manually recover the bot session and restart the gateway. That is a channel-level outage caused by a per-event decrypt failure. Keeping the monitor alive for this specific missing-key encrypted event lets later Matrix sync work continue while preserving fatal handling for real SDK or account failures.

## Scope boundary

- In scope: the Matrix monitor lifecycle decision that handles `sync.unexpected_error` and decides whether `waitForFatalStop()` should reject.
- In scope: one regression test for the issue-shaped missing-key encrypted event message.
- Out of scope: changing Matrix decrypt retry counts, key backup/session recovery, crypto storage, account verification, gateway routing, provider/model routing, plugin hooks, channel delivery semantics, and any public configuration/default/schema/protocol surface.
- Failure-mode boundary: only an error message that contains the encrypted event context (`error decrypting event` + `m.room.encrypted`) and the missing-key decrypt reason (`DecryptionError`/`MEGOLM_UNKNOWN_INBOUND_SESSION_ID` plus the missing key reason) is treated as nonfatal. Ordinary `sync.unexpected_error` remains fatal.

## Source-of-truth boundary

The source of truth for the outage is `createMatrixMonitorSyncLifecycle`: it subscribes to the Matrix SDK `sync.unexpected_error` event and currently calls `statusController.noteUnexpectedError(error)` plus `settleFatal(error)` for every such error. This patch changes only that lifecycle fatal-stop boundary. It does not move responsibility into downstream status rendering or upstream Matrix SDK/decrypt retry behavior.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- Related open PR: #94044 (`fix(matrix): prevent gateway stop on Megolm missing-key decryption failures`).
- Same user issue, different patch shape: #94044 classifies broad message buckets (`decrypt`, `megolm`, `network`, `timeout`, `offline`, `m_unknown_token`, `m_limit_exceeded`) as recoverable and also changes decrypt retry attempts for `MEGOLM_UNKNOWN_INBOUND_SESSION_ID`.
- Why this PR is intentionally narrower: the reported outage happens when an issue-shaped missing-key encrypted-event decrypt error reaches the monitor lifecycle fatal-stop path. This patch fixes that exact source-of-truth boundary without changing retry policy, network/token error handling, crypto state, or account recovery behavior.
- Why this is safer to review: the runtime proof below includes a negative-control ordinary `sync.unexpected_error` probe, which still rejects `waitForFatalStop()` and records `healthState: "error"`. That guards against turning unrelated Matrix sync, token, timeout, or SDK failures into silent recoverable events.
- Non-duplication claim: this is not a broader canonical retry-policy patch; it is the minimal lifecycle root-cause fix for #93501 with a local real-behavior proof of both the fixed missing-key path and the unchanged fatal path.

## Origin / follow-up

N/A — independent root-cause PR for #93501. #94044 is an external competing open PR for the same issue, not a merged origin/follow-up anchor.

Closes #93501

## Patch quality notes

- Diff size is intentionally small: one production file and one matching regression test file.
- The matcher is conjunctive instead of a broad `decrypt`/`megolm` substring allowlist, so unrelated SDK, token, timeout, network, and account errors are not reclassified.
- The patch does not add fallback state, retry loops, compatibility shims, public options, schema changes, or new gateway/channel behavior.
- The negative-control runtime probe and existing fatal-path test coverage explain the public-contract warning around `sync.unexpected_error`: the event contract still treats ordinary unexpected sync errors as fatal; only the specific missing-key encrypted-event failure is excluded.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Matrix missing-key encrypted-event decryption errors should not stop the Matrix channel monitor; unrelated unexpected sync errors must remain fatal.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux worktree at PR head `8411e6fc8bb88b816faa7b28bf2a5c77dbb905bf`, fetched `origin/main` `bdc46fa28de3364ed95d89e6916002ca159fcd6e`, Node `v22.22.0`, pnpm dependencies installed from the lockfile. The proof drives the Matrix monitor lifecycle at the SDK event boundary by emitting `sync.unexpected_error` on a Matrix-client-compatible event emitter.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm -C "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-93501" install --frozen-lockfile
```

```bash
cd "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-93501" && \
  node --import tsx "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-93501-evidence/matrix-missing-key-sync-proof.mjs" | \
  tee "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-93501-evidence/matrix-missing-key-sync-proof.output.json"
```

```bash
cd "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-93501" && \
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/sync-lifecycle.test.ts \
    extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/index.test.ts
```

```bash
git -C "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-93501" diff --check
```

- **Evidence after fix:** The Matrix missing-key scenario kept the fatal wait pending and produced no error status. The ordinary unexpected error probe still rejected and recorded an error.

```json
{
  "results": [
    {
      "scenario": "missing-key-decryption",
      "waitBeforeDispose": "pending",
      "errorStatusCount": 0,
      "lastHealthState": "starting"
    },
    {
      "scenario": "ordinary-unexpected-error",
      "waitResult": "rejected:sync exploded",
      "errorStatusCount": 1,
      "lastError": "sync exploded"
    }
  ]
}
```

The focused monitor suites passed:

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  52 passed (52)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 87.22s
```

`diff --check` passed with no output.

- **Observed result after fix:** The Matrix monitor lifecycle no longer treats the issue-shaped missing-key encrypted-event decryption error as a fatal channel stop, and the adjacent fatal error path remains unchanged for ordinary unexpected sync errors.
- **What was not tested:** No live production Matrix account, Element session, external homeserver, or full Docker-backed Matrix QA lane was used. The proof isolates the SDK `sync.unexpected_error` event boundary that caused the monitor task to stop.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix for the monitor fatal-stop boundary.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/sync-lifecycle.test.ts`
- **Regression scenario:** A `sync.unexpected_error` carrying `Error decrypting event ... type=m.room.encrypted ... DecryptionError ... has not sent us the keys` must not set Matrix monitor error health and must keep `waitForFatalStop()` alive until normal disposal.
- **Test guardrail rationale:** The same lifecycle suite already covers the fatal path for ordinary `sync.unexpected_error`; keeping that coverage plus the runtime negative-control probe prevents the fix from becoming a broad error-swallowing fallback.
- `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/index.test.ts` was run as the closest monitor integration coverage around status/lifecycle wiring.
- The regression test was added before the production fix and failed on the current implementation with `AssertionError: expected true to be false`, then passed after the lifecycle filter was added.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** `channel: matrix`, existing related PR #94044, message-delivery behavior, session-state health reporting, and external Matrix live-proof boundary.
- **Risk explanation:** The changed code is in the Matrix monitor lifecycle fatal-stop filter. An over-broad filter could hide real sync failures or make the channel look healthy when the SDK/account is actually broken.
- **Why acceptable:** The matcher is intentionally narrow to the issue-shaped missing-key encrypted-event error, and both the runtime proof and existing tests show unrelated unexpected sync errors still stop the monitor and record an error.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** `createMatrixMonitorSyncLifecycle` treated every `sync.unexpected_error` from the Matrix SDK as fatal. Missing-key encrypted-event decrypt errors are per-event recoverable failures already handled by Matrix decryption/failed-decryption paths, but the lifecycle layer converted them into a channel-level fatal stop.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The fix is applied at the lifecycle fatal-stop boundary where the channel was being stopped, rather than broadening decrypt retry behavior or changing Matrix account/crypto state.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the targeted lifecycle bug; live production Matrix proof is not included and is disclosed above.

target.title: feat(status): show session costs
target.body:
## Summary
- Use transcript `usage.cost.total` for `/status` cost when available, so the status card can show cumulative transcript-backed session cost instead of only a pricing-table estimate.
- Add a conditional `Cost` column to `openclaw status` recent session rows when at least one shown session has persisted cost.
- Update status/cost docs for the new behavior.

## Linked context
Fixes #64334.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
Behavior addressed: `/status` and `openclaw status` now surface existing session cost data.
Real environment tested: Local source checkout on macOS using an isolated temporary OpenClaw state directory with one session store and transcript; CLI launched with temporary Node v24.16.0 because the system Node v22.15.0 is below OpenClaw's runtime guard.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_HOME="/var/folders/6l/gn1ygj3d28s_pdrxjqkzc1440000gn/T/opencode/status-proof-94308/home" OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="/var/folders/6l/gn1ygj3d28s_pdrxjqkzc1440000gn/T/opencode/status-proof-94308/state" PATH="/var/folders/6l/gn1ygj3d28s_pdrxjqkzc1440000gn/T/opencode/corepack-bin:$PATH" corepack pnpm dlx node@24 openclaw.mjs status`
Evidence after fix: Real CLI output included the Sessions table below from the isolated OpenClaw state:

target.title: fix(models): resolve provider-qualified aliases in session switches
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

  - Problem: Control UI/TUI session model switching could pass a provider-qualified display alias like `lmstudio-moe/MoE` through `sessions.patch`, which let the provider receive `MoE` instead of the canonical local model id.
  - Why it matters: LM Studio and other local providers reject display aliases as invalid model identifiers, breaking user-selected mid-session model switches.
  - What changed: Shared model selection now resolves `provider/Alias` through the configured alias index when the provider matches the alias target, before existing allowlist/catalog validation and persistence.
  - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No fallback behavior, provider-specific LM Studio logic, Telegram behavior, or broad Control UI state handling changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #75163
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

  - Root cause: `resolveModelRefFromString` resolved aliases only when the entire raw model string matched a configured alias. Bare `MoE` resolved, but `lmstudio-moe/MoE` was parsed as provider `lmstudio-moe` and model `MoE`.
  - Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered bare aliases and slash-form aliases, but not provider-qualified display aliases emitted by session/model selection paths.
  - Contributing context (if known): Control UI/TUI session state can combine a server-reported model alias with its provider into `provider/Alias`, then send that value through `sessions.patch`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
  - Target test or file: `src/agents/model-selection.test.ts`, `src/gateway/sessions- patch.test.ts`
  - Scenario the test should lock in: `lmstudio-moe/MoE` resolves and persists as `lmstudio-moe/ qwen3.6-35b-a3b` when `MoE` is configured as that model’s alias.
  - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The resolver test locks the shared canonicalization rule; the Gateway patch test proves the user session persistence boundary stores the canonical override.
  - Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing tests covered bare aliases and explicit provider/model refs, but not provider-qualified aliases.
  - If no new test is added, why not: N/A


## User-visible / Behavior Changes

  Users can switch to configured aliased local/provider models from Control UI/TUI without the provider receiving the display alias as the model id.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
  Before:
  [model switch: lmstudio-moe/MoE] -> [sessions.patch stores/parses MoE as model] -> [provider
  rejects invalid model id]

  After:
  [model switch: lmstudio-moe/MoE] -> [shared alias resolver maps to qwen3.6-35b-a3b] ->
  [sessions.patch persists canonical override]
```

## Security Impact (required)

  - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) No
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No
  - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No
  - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No
  - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

  - OS: macOS local dev environment
  - Runtime/container: Node/pnpm workspace
  - Model/provider: LM Studio-style local provider alias (lmstudio-moe/MoE) covered by regression tests
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI/TUI session model switching via sessions.patch
  - Relevant config (redacted): agents.defaults.models["lmstudio-moe/qwen3.6-35b-a3b"].alias = "MoE"


### Steps

  1. Configure an allowed model with alias MoE for canonical ref lmstudio-moe/qwen3.6-35b-a3b.
  2. Send sessions.patch with model lmstudio-moe/MoE.
  3. Inspect the persisted session override.

### Expected

  - Session override persists provider lmstudio-moe and model qwen3.6-35b-a3b.

### Actual

  - Before this PR, lmstudio-moe/MoE was treated as direct model MoE, causing an invalid model identifier path.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

  - Verified scenarios:
      - pnpm test src/agents/model-selection.test.ts
      - pnpm test src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts
      - pnpm test src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts ui/src/ui/
        chat-model-ref.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat-model-select-state.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/
        chat.test.ts
      - pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/model-selection-shared.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.ts ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat-model-select-state.test.ts
      - pnpm build
      - git diff --check
      - pnpm dlx codex review --base origin/main
  - Edge cases checked: Existing slash-form alias coverage still passes; explicit allowlisted provider/model refs still pass; provider-qualified alias only resolves when the provider matches the alias target.
  - What you did not verify: Live LM Studio server behavior; Testbox pnpm check:changed was blocked because Blacksmith CLI was not authenticated and no BLACKSMITH_ORG was configured.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes
  - Config/env changes? (Yes/No) No
  - Migration needed? (Yes/No) No
  - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

  - Risk: A provider/Alias string could be mistaken for a real provider/model ref.
      - Mitigation: The new resolution only applies when the suffix matches a configured alias and the prefix provider matches the alias target provider; otherwise existing parsing and allowlist validation are preserved.

### Built with Codex

target.title: fix(gateway.tls): reject empty/whitespace certPath and keyPath
target.body:
## Summary

`gateway.tls.certPath` and `gateway.tls.keyPath` both accept `""` and whitespace-only strings at the schema layer (`z.string().optional()` with no `.min(1)`), and the runtime fallback `cfg.certPath ?? path.join(baseDir, "...")` only triggers on `null`/`undefined`, so empty strings reach `generateSelfSignedCert` unchanged. From there `path.dirname("") === "."` and `execFileAsync(openssl, [..., "-out", "", "-keyout", ""])` produces a cryptic openssl error that does not point at the real configuration mistake.

Sibling field `caPath` already guards against this via `cfg.caPath ? resolveUserPath(cfg.caPath) : undefined` (truthy check), so the fix brings `certPath`/`keyPath` to the same defensive style.

Three changes:

1. **Schema** (`src/config/zod-schema.ts:1108-1109`): Tighten to `z.string().trim().min(1).optional()` so the schema rejects `""` and whitespace-only paths at validation time.
2. **Runtime** (`src/infra/tls/gateway.ts:83-84`): Replace `??` with explicit `typeof === "string" && trim()` fallback so external callers that bypass the schema (tests, programmatic config) still get the default path. Aligns with the existing `caPath` pattern on line 85.
3. **chmod error visibility** (`src/infra/tls/gateway.ts:65-66`): Rethrow the chmod error instead of `.catch(() => {})` so any future path-related chmod failure surfaces with the offending path in the error message.

## Linked context

N/A (no existing issue)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `gateway.tls.certPath=""` passed schema validation and the `??` fallback did not trigger for empty strings, so `""` reached `generateSelfSignedCert` and produced a cryptic openssl error.
- **Real environment tested:** Node 22.19, Linux x86_64, local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` main (5372c7146b).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  node --import tsx /tmp/verify-tls-empty-path.mjs
  ```
  Script imports `loadGatewayTlsRuntime` from the patched source and verifies empty-string fallback, whitespace fallback, and the disabled-config regression guard.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture of `node` runtime verification (copied live output):

  ```
  === gateway.tls empty path runtime verification ===

  === Test 1: empty certPath/keyPath ===

    Input: certPath="", keyPath=""
    certPath resolved: /home/.../.openclaw/gateway/tls/gateway-cert.pem
    keyPath resolved:  /home/.../.openclaw/gateway/tls/gateway-key.pem
    certPath valid: PASS
    keyPath valid:  PASS

  === Test 2: whitespace-only paths ===

    Input: certPath="   ", keyPath="\t"
    certPath resolved: /home/.../.openclaw/gateway/tls/gateway-cert.pem
    keyPath resolved:  /home/.../.openclaw/gateway/tls/gateway-key.pem
    certPath valid: PASS
    keyPath valid:  PASS

  === Test 3: valid paths (regression guard) ===

    Input: enabled: false (no certPath/keyPath)
    enabled: false (expected: false)
    required: false (expected: false)
    PASS

  === ALL PASSED ===
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** Empty and whitespace-only `certPath`/`keyPath` now fall back to the default gateway TLS directory (`~/.openclaw/gateway/tls/gateway-cert.pem`) instead of passing `""` to openssl. The disabled-config path continues to work unchanged.
- **What was not tested:** End-to-end openssl execution with a real certificate generation (covered by the existing `loadGatewayTlsRuntime` tests which verify the full TLS load path).

## Tests and validation

```
$ pnpm test src/config/zod-schema.tls.test.ts src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts
✓ Test Files  2 passed (2)
✓ Tests      10 passed (10)
```

## Risk checklist

- [x] Does this change affect user-visible behavior? **Yes** — users who previously had empty or whitespace-only `certPath`/`keyPath` will now get the default gateway TLS path instead of a cryptic openssl error.
- [x] Does this change affect configuration? **Yes** — empty/whitespace strings for `certPath`/`keyPath` are now rejected by schema validation (`trim().min(1)`). This is backwards-compatible: empty paths previously produced obscure openssl failures; they now produce a clear validation error.
- [x] Does this change affect security? **No**
- [x] Does this change affect plugins or providers? **No**
- [x] Does this change affect docs? **No**
- [x] Is this change backwards-compatible? **Yes** — all previously-valid non-empty paths continue to work identically. Empty/whitespace paths that previously silently broke now either fail validation or fall back to default.

target.title: fix #94166: memory-core OpenAI-compatible embeddings: honor provider request.allowPrivateNetwork
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `memory-core` OpenAI-compatible embedding requests did not carry the selected configured provider's `request.allowPrivateNetwork` transport policy into the embedding guarded-fetch policy, so memory embeddings could not honor provider-level private-network trust consistently.
- **Solution:** Reuse the existing provider trust contract at the embedding SSRF policy owner: omitted `request.allowPrivateNetwork` preserves exact-host compatibility, explicit `true` overlays broader private-network trust, and explicit `false` opts out of exact-host private-network trust.
- **What changed:** `ConfiguredEmbeddingProvider` reads `request.allowPrivateNetwork`; OpenAI-compatible memory embeddings return no exact-host SSRF policy for explicit provider denial, keep `allowedHostnames` for omitted config, and add `allowPrivateNetwork: true` only for explicit provider opt-in. Regression coverage now locks all three states.
- **What did NOT change:** Other embedding providers, non-memory model-provider request paths, gateway/channel/session routing, global SSRF helpers, schema/config surface, provider request field names, migrations, and metadata/link-local default protections are unchanged.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Existing configured OpenAI-compatible memory provider aliases keep working by default, operators who intentionally need broader private-network trust can use the existing provider `request.allowPrivateNetwork` knob, and operators who explicitly set `request.allowPrivateNetwork: false` get the same denial semantics as the sibling model-provider transport path.
- Fixes #94166

## Origin / follow-up

N/A — independent root cause.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- **Linked PR(s):** #94180 (`feat(memory-core): allow private network endpoints for memory embeddings`). The public-contract helper also flagged #92307 through an environment/version term, but #92307 is about exec-approval startup warnings and is not the same embedding/private-network contract.
- **Gap in linked PR(s):** #94180 adds a new memory-specific config surface, `memorySearch.remote.network.dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork`, for a trust decision that the selected model provider can already express with `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork`.
- **Why this patch is better/canonical:** This patch reuses the existing provider transport-trust contract as the canonical source of truth for configured providers instead of introducing a second memory-only knob, while preserving shipped exact-host upgrade behavior for omitted config and honoring explicit provider denial.
- **Local real behavior proof advantage:** The local proof exercises the real `memory status --deep --json` CLI path against a loopback/private OpenAI-compatible endpoint in three states: omitted policy succeeds through exact-host trust with one `/v1/embeddings` request, explicit `request.allowPrivateNetwork: false` is blocked before the endpoint receives any request, and explicit `true` succeeds with one `/v1/embeddings` request.
- **Related open PR scan:** Open PR searches for `request.allowPrivateNetwork`, `memory-core allowPrivateNetwork`, and OpenAI-compatible private embedding endpoints found #94180 as the same-contract alternative; no broader canonical provider-inheritance PR was found besides the current PR.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw memory status --deep` against a configured OpenAI-compatible embedding endpoint on a loopback/private address must preserve exact-host compatibility when provider private-network policy is omitted, must block when the selected provider explicitly sets `request.allowPrivateNetwork: false`, and must honor `request.allowPrivateNetwork: true` for explicit broader private-network trust.
- **Real environment tested:** Live CLI proof was captured in the local PR worktree on Linux, using the real source CLI, the real `memory-core` CLI command, isolated `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and a local OpenAI-compatible `/v1/embeddings` HTTP endpoint bound to a loopback/private address with host and port redacted below.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** The same command was run three times with the same endpoint and separate isolated configs: omitted `models.providers.private-embedding-proof.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, explicit `request.allowPrivateNetwork: false`, and explicit `request.allowPrivateNetwork: true`.

```sh
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory status --agent proof --deep --json
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Exact-host compatibility result from the real CLI, with provider `request.allowPrivateNetwork` omitted:

```json
{
  "agentId": "proof",
  "status": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "requestedProvider": "private-embedding-proof",
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small"
  },
  "embeddingProbe": {
    "ok": true
  }
}
```

Exact-host compatibility summary:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "containsEmbeddingsReady": true,
  "serverRequestCount": 1
}
```

Explicit provider denial result from the real CLI:

```json
{
  "agentId": "proof",
  "status": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "requestedProvider": "private-embedding-proof",
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small"
  },
  "embeddingProbe": {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Blocked hostname or private/internal/special-use IP address"
  }
}
```

Explicit provider denial summary:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "containsGuardBlock": true,
  "containsEmbeddingsReady": false,
  "serverRequestCount": 0
}
```

Provider opt-in overlay result from the real CLI:

```json
{
  "agentId": "proof",
  "status": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "requestedProvider": "private-embedding-proof",
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small"
  },
  "embeddingProbe": {
    "ok": true
  }
}
```

Provider opt-in overlay summary:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "containsEmbeddingsReady": true,
  "serverRequestCount": 1
}
```

Redacted endpoint request observed once in the omitted case and once in the opt-in case; the explicit false case observed zero endpoint requests:

```json
{
  "method": "POST",
  "url": "/v1/embeddings",
  "headers": {
    "host": "<loopback>:<redacted-port>",
    "authorization": "<redacted>",
    "content-type": "application/json",
    "x-proof": "openclaw-pr-94344"
  },
  "body": {
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small",
    "input": ["ping"],
    "dimensions": 3
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The omitted configured provider alias reaches the loopback/private embedding endpoint successfully. The explicit false config is blocked by the guarded-fetch path before the endpoint sees a request. The explicit true config returns `embeddingProbe.ok: true` and reaches the endpoint once.
- **What was not tested:** I did not use a maintainer's real private LAN/VPN embedding service, third-party credentials, Mantis, Telegram, or Desktop proof infrastructure. This PR does not touch Telegram/Desktop/channel delivery; the local proof exercises the relevant memory CLI, configured-provider, and guarded-fetch runtime surface. I did not drive a live metadata/link-local endpoint for safety; metadata/link-local rebinding remains covered by the regression test that resolves an exact-host policy to `169.254.169.254` and expects private/internal blocking when broader opt-in is not set. I did not run `openclaw memory index --force`; `memory status --deep` is the requested memory runtime probe path and reaches the embedding availability check directly.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix

## Review findings addressed

| Finding | Status | Resolution |
| --- | --- | --- |
| RF-001 | Addressed | This update is the author-side response to the current `status: ⏳ waiting on author` label. |
| RF-002 | Fixed/proven | Explicit `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork: false` now returns no embedding exact-host SSRF policy; the live CLI proof shows `embeddingProbe.ok: false`, the private/internal block message, and zero endpoint requests. |
| RF-003 | Disclosed | Memory embeddings intentionally inherit provider-level private-network trust for explicit `true`; maintainer/security acceptance remains required because this is an SSRF trust-boundary policy. |
| RF-004 | Fixed/disclosed | The focused repair aligns explicit false semantics and tests; maintainer/security review remains the final gate for the provider trust inheritance decision. |
| RF-005 | Fixed/proven | `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.ts` now honors explicit provider denial before constructing exact-host trust, and the owner-module regression test locks that behavior. |
| RF-006 | Fixed/proven | `CI=true OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts --reporter=verbose` passed locally with 29/29 tests. |
| RF-007 | Fixed/proven | `CI=true OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts --reporter=verbose` passed locally with 14/14 tests. |

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test files:** `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts`; `src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts`
- **Scenario locked in:** Configured provider with `request.allowPrivateNetwork: true` sets `client.ssrfPolicy.allowedHostnames === ["llm.internal"]` and `client.ssrfPolicy.allowPrivateNetwork === true`; omitted config preserves exact-host trust and keeps `allowPrivateNetwork` unset; explicit `request.allowPrivateNetwork: false` returns no embedding SSRF policy and private/internal resolution remains blocked; configured local/private provider aliases work under omitted config; the gateway configured-provider route still reaches the local generic embedding provider.
- **Commands run after this patch:**

```sh
CI=true OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts --reporter=verbose
CI=true OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

- **Observed local result:** `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts` passed 29/29 tests; `src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts` passed 14/14 tests in the local PR worktree after the explicit false repair.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The unit tests pin the owner module's SSRF policy construction for false/omitted/true, the gateway test pins the configured provider HTTP embedding route, and the live CLI proof pins the runtime memory-core guarded-fetch path. Together they cover the exact source-of-truth boundary without adding a broader fixture or changing unrelated provider paths.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** security-boundary, auth-provider, compatibility, session-state.
- **Risk explanation:** This touches the security boundary for private-network embedding fetches and the provider/config contract that decides when exact-host or broader private-network trust is allowed. A wrong change could relax SSRF blocking by default, break existing configured local/private aliases, or create a second incompatible memory-only trust surface.
- **Why acceptable:** The diff is limited to the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider and its tests; shipped exact-host trust for omitted configured aliases is preserved; explicit false now denies exact-host private trust; metadata/link-local rebinding remains blocked without broader opt-in; broader private-network access stays behind explicit provider-scoped opt-in; no schema, migration, or new public config knob is introduced.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The provider `request.allowPrivateNetwork` contract is the existing source-of-truth for configured provider private-network transport trust, but the memory OpenAI-compatible embedding adapter previously built its SSRF policy from the embedding base URL without consuming the full configured provider request state. The first PR revision handled explicit `true`, but explicit `false` still fell through to exact-host trust.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The trust decision now lives where the embedding SSRF policy is created: explicit provider denial returns no exact-host policy, omitted config preserves the existing exact-host policy, and explicit provider opt-in overlays broader `allowPrivateNetwork` trust. This fixes source policy construction instead of masking blocked fetches downstream or adding a parallel memory-only override.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High — the patch stays focused on the embedding provider policy and configured-provider embedding test coverage, includes real CLI proof for the runtime path, preserves existing alias compatibility, handles explicit denial, and leaves the final product/security acceptance explicitly called out for maintainer review.
- **Patch quality notes:** Patch-quality warnings are expected and reviewed: the `unknown as EmbeddingProviderCreateOptions["config"]` casts are test fixture casts for partial config objects, not production type suppression; returning `undefined` for explicit false is the intentional denial path and matches the sibling model-provider behavior where no exact-origin trust is attached. No skipped tests, `.only`, lint disables, or runtime broad catch were added.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The canonical contract surface is `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork` for provider transport trust. This patch makes memory embedding SSRF policy consume that source-of-truth contract before guarded fetch runs; it does not change schema/default behavior, does not add migration or partial override semantics, and intentionally leaves other providers and non-memory request paths out of scope.

target.title: fix(embedded-agent): route inbound to run-now past stale non-streamin…
target.body:
## Problem

Inbound messages can be silently dropped until the gateway is restarted. A session stops responding: messages are received by the channel but never dispatched to the agent — no model call, nothing appended to history.

## Root cause

The reply dispatch gate decides `run-now` vs. `enqueue-followup` from bare presence in `ACTIVE_EMBEDDED_RUNS` (`isEmbeddedAgentRunActive` → `ACTIVE_EMBEDDED_RUNS.has`).

When a run's stream ends but its handle is not cleared — teardown only clears on a handle-identity match, so a replaced or non-timeout handle survives — the entry lingers and reads as active indefinitely. Subsequent inbounds are then routed into a followup queue whose drain is never scheduled, so they park forever.

`isStreaming()` is set `true` for the entire run (including tool-execution gaps) and only cleared when the run finishes, so it is a reliable liveness signal — and it is already computed alongside the presence check at both dispatch sites.

## Fix

Gate the queue-action decision on liveness (`isActive && isStreaming`) at both dispatch sites (`get-reply-run`, `agent-runner`). A genuinely busy run is unaffected; only a stale present-but-non-streaming entry now routes `run-now`.

The shared `isEmbeddedAgentRunActive` predicate is intentionally left untouched, so callers that rely on presence semantics (gateway status, stuck-session recovery) are unchanged. This keeps the change scoped to the dispatch decision.

## Testing

Adds a regression test asserting a stale (present, non-streaming) run runs now instead of enqueuing a followup. Reverting the fix makes the test fail (the run is enqueued and blackholed); `agent-runner.media-paths.test.ts` passes with it.
